# Grove/Doerr OKR Canon: Intellectual Lineage and Canonical Definition

## Summary

OKRs (Objectives and Key Results) originated at Intel under Andy Grove in the 1970s as an evolution of Peter Drucker's Management by Objectives (MBO). Grove's crucial innovation was separating the "what" (Objective) from the "how" (Key Results), and insisting that Key Results be measurable, time-bound outcomes rather than activities. John Doerr, who learned OKRs directly from Grove as an Intel engineer, brought the framework to Google in 1999 and later formalized it in his 2018 book "Measure What Matters." Google's adoption - starting with ~40 employees and scaling to 140,000+ - is the most cited evidence for OKR effectiveness at scale.

The canonical OKR definition from Doerr: an Objective answers the question "What do I want to accomplish?" and should be "significant, concrete, action-oriented, and (ideally) inspirational." Key Results answer "How will I know if I've gotten there?" and must be "specific and time-bound, aggressive yet realistic. Most of all, they must be measurable and verifiable." A KR is not a task ("launch the new feature") but a result ("achieve 10,000 daily active users of the new feature").

Grove's formulation from "High Output Management" (1983): "The key result has to be measurable. But at the end you can look, and without any arguments: did I do that or did I not do it? Yes? No? Simple. No judgments in it." This binary-or-graded measurability test is the single most important OKR quality gate.

## Key quotations / statistics

- Andy Grove, High Output Management (1983): "The key result has to be measurable. But at the end you can look, and without any arguments: did I do that or did I not do it?"
- John Doerr, Measure What Matters (2018): "An Objective, I explained, is simply WHAT is to be achieved, no more and no less. By definition, objectives are significant, concrete, action oriented, and (ideally) inspirational... Key results benchmark and monitor HOW we get to the objective. Effective KRs are specific and time-bound, aggressive yet realistic. Most of all, they must be measurable and verifiable."
- Doerr on the compensation prohibition: "OKRs are not a legal document upon which to base a performance review... When financial reward attaches to OKR outcomes, gaming and sandbagging ensue."
- Google re:Work: "OKRs are not a performance evaluation tool." - This is the foundational principle behind the compensation-linkage prohibition.
- Typical OKR count: 3-5 Key Results per Objective; no more than 3-5 Objectives per team per quarter.

## Annotations for stinger-forge

- This source grounds `guides/01-okr-canon.md`. The Grove/Doerr lineage should be traced: Drucker MBO (1954) -> Grove Intel OKR adaptation (1970s, published 1983) -> Doerr Google introduction (1999) -> "Measure What Matters" formalization (2018).
- The compensation prohibition is a CRITICAL DIRECTIVE in the Command Brief and must be the first principle in `guides/00-principles.md`.
- The binary measurability test from Grove ("did I do that or did I not do it?") is the cornerstone of the output-vs-input KR discipline described in `guides/03-writing-key-results.md`.
- Google re:Work is the most accessible free canonical source; it should be cited throughout the stinger as an official reference alongside the books.
